---
title: "Study Design"
subtitle: "Motivation, data, evaluation protocol, and search procedure"
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
variables = FileAttachment("_data/variable_metadata.json").json()
```

## Research question

Can an LLM-guided search procedure improve pseudo-real-time forecasts of the Norwegian macroeconomy by selecting data representations, covariates, and fine-tuning settings for a time series foundation model?

We test this by running an automated search loop over the pipeline configuration of **Chronos-2** (120M parameters), a pretrained time series foundation model, and comparing against classical baselines under a strict rolling pseudo-real-time evaluation protocol.

## Hypotheses

| | Hypothesis | Status |
|---|-----------|--------|
| H1 | Zero-shot Chronos-2 improves on classical baselines for some series | Partially confirmed (h=1 competitive, longer horizons fall behind) |
| H2 | Economy-specific adaptation improves on zero-shot | Confirmed (covariates reduce MASE by 6.6%) |
| H3 | LLM-guided search discovers better setups than manual selection | Under investigation |
| H4 | Most gains come from covariate selection, not fine-tuning | Confirmed so far (fine-tuning hurts, covariates help) |


## Data

The panel combines **18 macroeconomic variables** from three sources, covering Norwegian domestic indicators, central bank data, and global macro variables.

### Variable panel

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(variables.map(v => ({
  Variable: v.display_name,
  Source: v.source,
  "Pub. lag": `${v.publication_lag_days}d`,
  Target: v.is_target ? "Yes" : "",
  Description: v.description,
  From: v.first_available ? v.first_available.slice(0, 10) : "—"
})), {
  sort: "Source",
  rows: 20,
  width: {Variable: 160, Source: 100, "Pub. lag": 60, Target: 45, Description: 250, From: 85}
})
```

### Pseudo-real-time discipline

At each forecast origin *t*, only data published by date *t* is used. For an observation from month *m*:

> The observation is available when: **end-of-month(*m*) + publication lag ≤ *t***

This prevents future information leakage. Publication lags range from 0 days (policy rate, known in real time) to 90 days (Euro area GDP). Daily series are aggregated to monthly averages; quarterly series are forward-filled.


## Evaluation protocol

### Rolling expanding-window design

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-protocol
//| fig-cap: "Evaluation timeline. Each row represents a forecast origin. The model uses all available data up to the origin (blue) and produces forecasts at h=1,3,6,12 months ahead (orange dots)."

{
  // Illustrative diagram: show 5 sample origins
  const origins = [
    {origin: new Date("2006-06-30"), label: "2006-06"},
    {origin: new Date("2009-01-31"), label: "2009-01"},
    {origin: new Date("2012-06-30"), label: "2012-06"},
    {origin: new Date("2015-12-31"), label: "2015-12"},
    {origin: new Date("2020-01-31"), label: "2020-01"},
  ];

  const horizons = [1, 3, 6, 12];
  const data = [];

  for (const o of origins) {
    // Training bar
    data.push({
      origin: o.label,
      x1: new Date("1990-01-01"),
      x2: o.origin,
      type: "Training data"
    });
    // Forecast points
    for (const h of horizons) {
      const target = new Date(o.origin);
      target.setMonth(target.getMonth() + h);
      data.push({
        origin: o.label,
        x: target,
        h: h,
        type: "Forecast"
      });
    }
  }

  const bars = data.filter(d => d.type === "Training data");
  const dots = data.filter(d => d.type === "Forecast");

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 220,
    marginLeft: 80,
    marginBottom: 40,
    x: {type: "utc", label: "Date", domain: [new Date("1988-01-01"), new Date("2022-01-01")]},
    y: {label: "Origin", domain: origins.map(o => o.label).reverse()},
    marks: [
      // Validation / test boundary
      Plot.ruleX([new Date("2016-01-01")], {stroke: "#e74c3c", strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
      Plot.text([{x: new Date("2016-01-01"), text: "Test era →"}], {
        x: "x", text: "text", dy: -8, dx: 5, fontSize: 9, fill: "#e74c3c", textAnchor: "start"
      }),
      // Training bars
      Plot.barX(bars, {
        x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y: "origin",
        fill: "#3498db", fillOpacity: 0.4
      }),
      // Origin markers
      Plot.dot(origins.map(o => ({x: o.origin, y: o.label})), {
        x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#2c3e50", r: 5
      }),
      // Forecast dots
      Plot.dot(dots, {
        x: "x", y: "origin",
        fill: d => ({"1": "#f39c12", "3": "#e67e22", "6": "#d35400", "12": "#c0392b"})[d.h],
        r: 4,
        tip: true, title: d => `h=${d.h}`
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

| Era | Period | Origins | Purpose |
|-----|--------|---------|---------|
| Validation | 2006-01 to 2015-12 | 120 monthly | Search, model selection |
| Test | 2016-01 onward | ~110 monthly | Final out-of-sample (frozen) |

Test-era subperiods: pre-COVID (2016-19), COVID (2020-21), post-COVID/inflation (2022+).


## Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| **RMSE** | √(mean((y - ŷ)²)) | Penalizes large errors; in original units |
| **MAE** | mean(\|y - ŷ\|) | Average absolute error; in original units |
| **MASE** | MAE / MAE_naive | Scale-free; < 1 beats random walk |

**MASE** is the primary search metric because it is comparable across variables with different scales.


## Baselines

| Method | Specification |
|--------|---------------|
| Random walk | ŷ_{t+h} = y_t (last observed value) |
| Seasonal naive | ŷ_{t+h} = y_{t+h-12} (same month last year) |
| AR(p) | Direct OLS, lag order by BIC, p ∈ {1,...,12} |
| ARIMA(p,d,q) | AIC grid search: p ∈ {0-3}, d ∈ {0-1}, q ∈ {0-2} |
| ETS | Auto-selected trend/seasonal via AIC |


## Foundation model: Chronos-2

**amazon/chronos-2** (120M parameters) — a universal time series transformer pretrained on billions of observations.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Parameters | 120M |
| Architecture | Transformer (universal) |
| Context length | Up to 8,192 time steps |
| Covariate support | Past covariates + known future covariates (native) |
| Fine-tuning | LoRA (r=8, α=16) via AutoGluon |
| Interface | AutoGluon `"Chronos-2"` model key |

Unlike earlier Chronos-Bolt models, Chronos-2 natively integrates covariate information during inference — making covariate selection a meaningful search dimension.


## Search procedure

An LLM (**Claude Sonnet**) proposes pipeline configurations based on domain knowledge and past results. Each iteration:

1. Claude reads: task description, search space, current best config, iteration history
2. Claude proposes a JSON config (which covariates, transforms, context length, fine-tuning)
3. **Quick evaluation** on 20 subsampled origins (~30s)
4. If improved: **full evaluation** on all 120 origins (~3 min)
5. Accept if full score improves; reject otherwise
6. Log iteration, update state, continue

### Search space

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const space = [
    {Dimension: "Covariates", Range: "Any subset of 14 variables", Default: "None"},
    {Dimension: "Covariate transforms", Range: "none, log_diff, pct_change_1/12, standardize, MA", Default: "None"},
    {Dimension: "Context length", Range: "24, 36, 48, 64, 96, 128, unlimited", Default: "Unlimited"},
    {Dimension: "Fine-tuning", Range: "Off / LoRA", Default: "Off"},
    {Dimension: "Fine-tune steps", Range: "100, 500, 1000, 2000", Default: "1000"},
    {Dimension: "Fine-tune LR", Range: "1e-6 to 1e-4 (log scale)", Default: "1e-5"},
    {Dimension: "Grouping", Range: "Univariate / All targets", Default: "Univariate"},
  ];
  return Inputs.table(space, {rows: 10, width: {Dimension: 160, Range: 280, Default: 100}});
}
```

Target variables are never transformed — forecasts must be in the original scale for evaluation.


## Reproducibility

| Component | Version / Setting |
|-----------|------------------|
| Python | 3.11 |
| chronos-forecasting | 2.2.2 |
| autogluon.timeseries | 1.5.0 |
| PyTorch | 2.9.1 (CUDA 12.8) |
| Hardware | NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB) |
| Origin subsample seed | 42 |
| Dependency lock | `uv.lock` |

All data sources are publicly available. The Anthropic API key is required only for the search loop.

::: {.callout-note}
Full details including table IDs, series codes, and formula notation are in [METHODOLOGY.md](../METHODOLOGY.md).
:::
